notable.notable_blurb: Co-founded Skull and Bones with Alphonso Taft, 1832

narrative_blocks.header: Co-founded Skull and Bones with Alphonso Taft
narrative_blocks.body: In 1832 at Yale, he co-founded Skull and Bones with Alphonso Taft — both Yale class of 1833, both Hooker descendants. The senior secret society became the most influential of its kind in American history, with members later including three Presidents, four Supreme Court justices, and numerous senators and cabinet members.

narrative_blocks.header: Second cousin to America's biggest opium trader
narrative_blocks.body: His second cousin Samuel Wadsworth Russell was the largest American opium trading house operator in China through the 1820s-30s. The Russell family fortune that helped support Skull and Bones's early decades came partly from Russell & Company's Canton operations.

narrative_blocks.header: Donated his ancestor's sea chest, 1888
narrative_blocks.body: In 1888 he donated his ancestor William Russell's sea chest to the New Haven Colony Historical Society — preserving a tangible 18th-century Russell-family artifact. The chest later passed through the family with George Huntington Larned loaning it back for further display.

narrative_blocks.header: Father of ten including Talcott Huntington Russell
narrative_blocks.body: He married Mary Elizabeth Hubbard on 29 August 1836 at Clinton NY; they had ten children including Talcott Huntington Russell. His son carried the Talcott + Russell + Huntington surname chain — three founding-era Connecticut family names compressed into one given-name string.

education.notes: — Yale 1833. Co-founded Skull and Bones 1832 with Alphonso Taft. Son of Matthew Talcott Russell (H00856). Second cousin of Samuel Wadsworth Russell (China trader).

cross_connections.display_label: Carried the Talcott name third generation, after grandfather and aunt.

notable.primary_url_label: Yale Library MS 1213 Russell Process Company Records

institutions.notes: Brother Edward H. Russell the Russell Process inventor was general manager.

narrative_blocks.header: Donated his ancestor's 1638 sea chest to history
narrative_blocks.body: In 1888 his family donated the William Russell sea chest, dating to 1638, to the New Haven Colony Historical Society. The provenance letter named him as the living end of a 250-year chain.

narrative_blocks.header: Father co-founded Skull and Bones at Yale
narrative_blocks.body: His father William Huntington Russell co-founded the secret society at Yale in 1832. The family's Yale connections ran through both the Russell and the Talcott lines for generations.

narrative_blocks.header: Third generation to carry the Talcott name
narrative_blocks.body: His grandfather Matthew Talcott Russell had been named for the childless Col. Matthew Talcott who funded his education. He was the third in his line to carry the Talcott name forward.

narrative_blocks.header: First chairman of CT workmen's compensation 1913
narrative_blocks.body: In 1911 he was retained by the CT legislative committee to draft workmen's compensation legislation; his draft 'formed the framework of much of the legislation finally adopted.' When Connecticut adopted the system in 1913 he was made first chairman of the commission — resigning 1.5 years later due to ill health.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale Law instructor on municipal corporations
narrative_blocks.body: From 1892 until 1900 he served as instructor on municipal corporations at the Yale School of Law — an 8-year teaching tenure during his New Haven private-practice years. The position followed his New Haven Board of Councilmen presidency in the city's municipal-government era.

narrative_blocks.header: Secretary-treasurer of the Russell Process Company
narrative_blocks.body: Together with his brother Edward — inventor of the Russell Process for silver extraction from low-grade ores — he ran the New York-headquartered Russell Process Company that licensed metallurgy patents through the 1880s-1890s western US silver mining boom. Much of the company's business was conducted out of his New Haven law office.

narrative_blocks.header: Conference on Uniform State Laws founding treasurer
narrative_blocks.body: He was one of the first members + treasurer of the Conference on Uniform State Laws (now the Uniform Law Commission) — the body that drafts model legislation for adoption by US states. He chaired the Commercial Law Committee, shaping early uniform commercial law jurisprudence.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale 1869, Columbia Law LL.B. 1872
narrative_blocks.body: Prepared at his father's school (General Russell's Collegiate and Commercial Institute) and at Lawrence Academy in Groton MA. After Yale BA 1869 he studied 1 year at Yale Law before transferring to Columbia Law School, earning his LL.B. 1872 — that year was admitted to the Connecticut bar.

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT

education.notes: Groton MA (Sam-Sr's school + his own preparatory)
Studied 1 year at Yale Law before transferring to Columbia.

career.location: New Haven CT office (NYC company HQ)
career.notes: Brother Edward H. Russell was general manager + inventor; C.A. Stetefeldt was president.
First chairman when CT adopted workmen's compensation 1913; resigned after 1.5 years due to ill health.

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT (alongside husband Talcott)

parents.father_name: Captain William W. Low, U.S. Navy

narrative_blocks.header: Navy captain's daughter who married a Yale lawyer
narrative_blocks.body: Daughter of Captain William W. Low, U.S. Navy, and Evelina (Peck) Low. Married Talcott Huntington Russell — Russell Process Company secretary-treasurer + workmen's compensation reformer — at New Haven on 10 December 1889.

burial.plot_notes: Sparkman Hillcrest Memorial Park, Dallas TX

narrative_blocks.header: Yale 1913 son of workmen's compensation reformer
narrative_blocks.body: Eldest son of Talcott Huntington Russell (Yale 1869 + Columbia Law 1872 + Russell Process Company secretary-treasurer + first chairman of CT Workmen's Compensation Commission). Yale BA 1913. Moved to Dallas Texas where he died in 1971 at 79 — buried Sparkman Hillcrest Memorial Park.

education.notes: Per Yale Obituary Record — Class of 1920.

narrative_blocks.header: Named for his Navy-captain maternal grandfather
narrative_blocks.body: Younger son of Talcott Huntington Russell + Geraldine Whittemore Low Russell. Named 'William Low' for his maternal grandfather Captain William W. Low USN.

sources.label: Yale University Obituary Record — Edward Hubbard Russell (Ph.B. 1878)
Yale Library Manuscripts MS 1213 — Russell Process Company Records

notable.primary_url_label: Yale Library MS 1213 Russell Process Company Records

institutions.notes: Russell Process for silver extraction from low-grade ores — basis of company.

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT

education.notes: Transferred 1879 from Yale College to Class of 1878 S. Member Sigma Delta Chi.
One year of post-bachelor's metallurgy study at Sheffield.

career.notes: First mining engineer position post-Sheffield; western US silver-mining boom.
Patents 1880, 1883, 1884, 1885, 1886, 1889 for silver extraction + ore leaching + hyposulphites + soda ash purification.
Gave up mining 1895; lived abroad until May 1928. Studied sociology + worked with poorer classes.

narrative_blocks.header: Invented the Russell Process for silver extraction
narrative_blocks.body: Around 1884 he invented the Russell Process — a hydro-metallurgical leaching method using sodium hyposulphite that allowed economic silver extraction from low-grade ores previously uneconomic. The process built on the older Austrian Patera method but achieved higher extraction rates and could handle ores with heavy base metals.

narrative_blocks.header: Patents across nine years, 1880-1889
narrative_blocks.body: He held US patents granted in 1880, 1883, 1884, 1885, 1886, and 1889 for separating metals from ores, leaching, purifying hyposulphites, and purifying soda ash. The process became highly lucrative for western US and Mexican silver-mining operations through the late 1880s.

narrative_blocks.header: Mining engineer at Park City, then London sociologist
narrative_blocks.body: After Yale Sheffield 1878 he worked 15-20 years as mining engineer in Park City Utah and across US + Mexico — including with the Ontario Mining Company. Gave up mining 1895 and lived in London until May 1928, devoting himself to the study of sociology + work among poorer classes.

narrative_blocks.header: Unmarried; died New Haven 1928 age seventy-two
narrative_blocks.body: He never married; on returning to New Haven May 1928 from his 33-year London period he died at the Parkhurst Sanitarium November 1928 of arteriosclerosis at age 72. Buried at Grove Street Cemetery in his birth city — alongside many of his Russell siblings.

cross_connections.display_label: His childless uncle paid for his Yale education and gave his name.
His grandson carried the Talcott given name forward to a third generation.

narrative_blocks.header: His childless uncle paid for his Yale education
narrative_blocks.body: Col. Matthew Talcott had married his aunt Mary Russell and they had no children of their own. The Colonel met the expenses of his namesake nephew's schooling at Yale.

narrative_blocks.header: Studied law under future Chief Justice Ellsworth
narrative_blocks.body: He read law in Windsor with Oliver Ellsworth, who would later draft the Constitution and serve as third Chief Justice of the United States. He was admitted to the Connecticut bar in January 1786.

narrative_blocks.header: His son co-founded Skull and Bones at Yale
narrative_blocks.body: His fifth son William Huntington Russell graduated Yale in 1833. William had co-founded the Skull and Bones secret society there in 1832 as an undergraduate.

narrative_blocks.header: Sixteen years as Middletown City Treasurer
narrative_blocks.body: He held the office continuously while serving as State's Attorney for Middlesex County. He was ordained Deacon of the church in April 1798.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale tutor before settling into law
narrative_blocks.body: He served as Yale tutor from November 1782 until May 1786. He was admitted to the College Church in 1785 during this period.

cross_connections.display_label: Married Gov. Talcott's granddaughter Esther Talcott in 1758

narrative_blocks.header: Yale 1750 — entire ministry at one church
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated Yale in 1750, was ordained at the First Church of Thompson Connecticut in 1757, and served there until his death in 1795. Thirty-eight years in one parish, his entire ministerial career.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Gov. Talcott's granddaughter — Hooker-Talcott convergence
narrative_blocks.body: He married Esther Talcott in 1758 — daughter of Joseph Talcott Jr., Treasurer of the Colony of Connecticut, and granddaughter of Gov. Joseph Talcott. This Russell-Talcott marriage echoed the convergence begun by his sister Mary Russell Talcott.

narrative_blocks.header: Apoplexy at Rev. Caleb Alexander's house, Mendon
narrative_blocks.body: He traveled to Boston for his health in October 1795 but was seized with apoplexy en route. He died on the evening of October 27 at the house of Rev. Caleb Alexander in Mendon, Massachusetts.

narrative_blocks.header: His grandson co-founded Yale's Skull and Bones
narrative_blocks.body: His son Matthew Talcott Russell named a son William Huntington Russell, who at Yale in 1832 co-founded the secret senior society Skull and Bones with Alphonso Taft. The line connects this Thompson Connecticut pulpit to the most secretive of American institutions.

INST022.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.notes: First American foreign-mission organization. Tree connections: Rev. Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.
